perception.who_is_this_for_a: Primarily individual used-car buyers who want a fast discounted official Carfax report, with dealers supported through a secondary volume-pricing link and later section.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: Dealers and volume buyers first, with a clearly visible fast lane for individuals who need just one report.

reasoning: Candidate A is very strong for the one-time buyer path: the first viewport clearly says official Carfax reports, shows $9.99 pricing, VIN entry, a buy CTA, refund/payment/delivery reassurances, savings, a dealer link, and a visible sample-style report preview. It feels fast, practical, and lower-friction on mobile. Its weakness is that the dealer path is visually subordinate in the first viewport and reads more like a supporting link than a strategically important higher-LTV path. Candidate B better matches the hybrid business goal: the first desktop viewport creates an explicit dealer-vs-one-report split, gives dealers serious prominence, includes dealer CTAs, and still provides a clear individual purchase card with VIN entry, price, guarantee, official PDF language, and sample-report link. It also has stronger perceived business legitimacy through authorized-reseller language, dealership volume claims, trust strip, dealer pricing tiers, business identity, and dealer setup flow. B is weaker on mobile because the header appears cramped/cropped and the dealer CTA is pushed lower, but overall it preserves both dealer seriousness and consumer immediacy better than A.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Dealer access is present but underweighted in the first viewport; it can feel like an individual-purchase page with dealer pricing as a secondary link.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Mobile header/navigation appears cramped or partially cut off, and the dealer conversion path is less immediately actionable on the first mobile screen.
